stories.headline: Medical examiner rules DIA runway death a suicide; security questions loom
stories.summary:
Denver's chief medical examiner ruled Tuesday that Michael Mott, 41, died by suicide after being struck by a Frontier Airlines plane at Denver International Airport late Friday. Mott scaled an 8-foot security fence topped with barbed wire and walked onto an active runway before being hit by a departing Los Angeles-bound flight, according to airport officials.

The collision sparked an engine fire and created a 4,000-foot debris field. Of the 231 passengers and crew who evacuated via emergency slides, 12 reported minor injuries and five were taken to the hospital; four of those had been released by Tuesday morning.

A radar system on the perimeter fence detected movement and triggered an alarm at 11:10 p.m., but the operator on duty attributed the alert to a nearby herd of deer and did not identify the intruder. Roughly 10 minutes later, the airport was notified that a pedestrian had been struck on the runway approximately 650 feet from the fence line.

DIA CEO Phil Washington said safety remains the airport's top priority, and aviation security expert Jeffrey Price of Metropolitan State University of Denver called the incident 'extraordinarily rare,' adding that it could prompt a broad reassessment of airport perimeter security standards nationwide. The FAA, TSA, Denver Police, Frontier Airlines and DIA are all participating in the investigation.

stories.headline: Elderly bystander shot in Commerce City; one suspect arrested, second at large
stories.summary:
Commerce City police are searching for a second shooting suspect after an 86-year-old woman was struck by gunfire Monday while sitting in a car near East 60th Avenue and Monaco Street. Officers arrested one suspect who fled on foot from the scene; a second suspect escaped in a vehicle and remains at large.

Authorities described the victim as an innocent bystander with no connection to the shooting. She was transported to a hospital for treatment, and her condition had not been publicly released as of Monday evening.

Police issued a shelter-in-place order for residents near the intersection while officers searched the area, but lifted the order approximately an hour later after the first suspect was taken into custody. No vehicle description for the second suspect was released.

stories.headline: Colorado Legislature sends TABOR ballot question to voters, directing billions toward K-12 education
stories.summary:
Colorado Democrats gave final approval Tuesday to Senate Bill 135, which places a question before voters in November asking whether the state may retain Taxpayer's Bill of Rights refunds to significantly boost K-12 education funding. If approved, the measure would raise the annual TABOR spending cap by an amount equal to the state's highest single-year K-12 expenditure — currently about $4.6 billion — though the full impact would be phased in over at least a decade.

The surplus retained by the state would first be used to increase K-12 spending by 2 percent over the prior year — roughly $107.4 million in the first year — with funds earmarked for teacher pay, class-size reduction and workforce preparation. Any remaining surplus would be divided between school districts and early childhood programs such as preschool access and childcare.

The measure was narrowed during the legislative process to ensure all retained funds go directly to children's programs rather than broader state priorities. State Sen. Jeff Bridges, a Greenwood Village Democrat and lead sponsor, called it 'billions more for K-12 public education.'

In the near term, the governor's office projects the state will exceed the existing TABOR cap by about $711 million and $516 million in the next two fiscal years, some of which is already committed to property tax relief for seniors. Coloradans will have the final say on the measure in November.

stories.headline: Federal judge orders ICE to retrain Colorado officers after finding repeated violations of warrant rules
stories.summary:
U.S. District Judge R. Brooke Jackson ruled Tuesday that Immigration and Customs Enforcement must retrain its arresting officers in Colorado after finding the agency had materially violated his earlier court order limiting warrantless arrests. The 60-page ruling also requires ICE to improve documentation of warrantless arrests, provide more records to immigrants' attorneys and pay their legal fees.

Jackson found that ICE agents in Colorado continued making warrantless arrests without first conducting individualized determinations of flight risk as required by federal law and the court's preliminary injunction, which he issued in November. At a March hearing, ICE agents acknowledged they had not been trained on how to comply with the order.

The case was brought by attorneys from the ACLU of Colorado and two law firms, representing plaintiffs including a 19-year-old University of Utah student who was detained after a Mesa County deputy stopped her and questioned her about her accent, and a 53-year-old construction worker with no criminal history arrested at a job site while agents were looking for someone else.

Jackson's order cited an 'inadequate understanding' among deportation officers of their legal obligations, finding that arrests claimed to be made pursuant to field warrants were in fact warrantless. The ruling is the latest legal check on the Trump administration's mass deportation efforts in Colorado.

stories.headline: Democrats drop effort to counter natural gas 'right' ballot initiative as Colorado session nears end
stories.summary:
Colorado Democrats abandoned their attempt to blunt a proposed ballot initiative that would give residents a legal right to use natural gas, as the legislature entered its final days of the 2026 session. The proposed initiative, which has not yet qualified for the November ballot, has drawn sharp opposition from clean energy advocates and Democrats who argue it could undermine state climate goals.

The move came as lawmakers also advanced a separate bill aimed at countering Initiative 175, which would require the state to spend more than $500 million annually on road funding currently directed to other priorities. House Bill 1430, introduced specifically to neutralize the initiative's budget impact, cleared Senate debate and is expected to receive a final vote on the last day of session Wednesday.

The final legislative days also featured a floor controversy when a Colorado Springs Republican lawmaker referred to George Floyd using a derogatory term and claimed Floyd died of a drug overdose during debate on Senate Bill 190, a policing accountability measure. The comments drew immediate condemnation from Democrats.

stories.headline: Bill to delay Colorado coal plant closures by three years heads to Gov. Polis
stories.summary:
Legislation sitting on Gov. Jared Polis' desk would allow Colorado Springs Utilities to keep the Ray Nixon Power Plant operating until 2032, three years beyond the 2029 deadline established under current state law. Colorado's existing statute requires coal-fired plants to shut down as part of a commitment to reduce greenhouse gas emissions 80 percent by 2030.

Colorado Springs Utilities officials say the extension would save approximately $200 million by giving the utility more time to develop renewable energy alternatives and avoiding costly new electric transmission infrastructure, which runs about $2 million per mile. Those savings, they argue, would prevent significant rate increases for customers in the Colorado Springs service area.

The governor's office said Polis is pleased that the utility and conservation advocates reached a 'tough but fair compromise,' and that the legislation includes requirements for annual progress reports to the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment. Polis has not yet signed the bill.

stories.headline: Broncos open 2026 NFL season on Monday Night Football against Chiefs at Arrowhead
stories.summary:
The Denver Broncos will open the 2026 NFL regular season on Monday Night Football, traveling to Arrowhead Stadium to face the Kansas City Chiefs on Sept. 14 with a 6:15 p.m. kickoff. The matchup pits the defending AFC West champion Broncos against the team they supplanted after Kansas City's nine-year run atop the division.

The game carries significant intrigue around the health of both starting quarterbacks. Chiefs quarterback Patrick Mahomes is recovering from a torn ACL and LCL sustained in December, and recent reporting suggests he may or may not be ready for Week 1. Broncos quarterback Bo Nix recently underwent a second ankle procedure and will be limited during the offseason program, though coach Sean Payton expects him to be fully cleared before training camp opens in July.

Payton is 4-2 against Kansas City since arriving in Denver and swept the Chiefs last season as the Broncos finished 14-3. Denver bolstered its offense this offseason by trading a first-round pick for receiver Jaylen Waddle, while the Chiefs have undergone significant roster turnover, trading cornerback Trent McDuffie to the Rams and losing other contributors to free agency.

stories.headline: Broncos extend GM George Paton through 2032 season
stories.summary:
The Denver Broncos have signed general manager George Paton to a new five-year contract extension that will keep him with the organization through the 2032 season, according to NFL Network insider Tom Pelissero. The deal cements Paton's role as the architect of a Broncos roster that finished 14-3 last season and captured the AFC's top seed.

Coach Sean Payton praised the working relationship, saying Paton is 'very good at what he does' and that the two complement each other. The extension comes as the Broncos are widely regarded as one of the NFL's top franchises after years of struggles following Peyton Manning's retirement.

Paton has overseen key moves including the drafting of Bo Nix and the recent trade for receiver Jaylen Waddle. His extended tenure signals the organization's intent to maintain continuity as Denver attempts to defend its AFC West title and make a Super Bowl run.

stories.headline: Nuggets decide against trading Nikola Jokic following first-round playoff exit
stories.summary:
The Denver Nuggets have made clear they will not trade star center Nikola Jokic following the team's first-round playoff elimination, according to reports. The Nuggets were bounced from the postseason in the first round, and team executive Josh Kroenke addressed the media to outline the organization's offseason direction.

The end-of-season press conference also raised questions about Jamal Murray's role going forward after an uneven postseason. Coach David Adelman is expected to return for another season as the franchise begins a period of self-evaluation following the disappointing result.

The Nuggets are projected to hold a draft pick that could land them a prospect in the mold of Peyton Watson in the 2026 NBA Draft. Denver's offseason will center on determining what supporting pieces can be added or adjusted around Jokic to restore the team's championship contention.

stories.headline: IonQ opens $100 million quantum computing lab in Boulder, expects to create 150 jobs
stories.summary:
Quantum computing company IonQ has opened a $100 million research and development facility in Boulder, Colorado, with plans to hire 150 workers. Gov. Jared Polis and Boulder Mayor Aaron Brockett attended and offered support for the project, which positions Colorado as a growing hub for quantum technology.

Boulder has long been home to federal research institutions including NIST and NOAA as well as the University of Colorado, making it an attractive location for advanced technology investment. The IonQ facility represents one of the largest single quantum computing investments in the region.

The opening is part of a broader trend of high-tech firms expanding in the Denver-Boulder corridor, drawn by the area's research talent and government partnerships.

stories.headline: Wood Partners breaks ground on 252-unit apartment development in Longmont
stories.summary:
Wood Partners and European investment firm EIG EuroInvestor have broken ground on Alta Longmont, a 252-unit apartment development in Longmont that is expected to open in spring 2028. The project adds to a wave of multifamily construction across the Denver metro as developers respond to persistent housing demand.

Longmont has emerged as a relatively affordable alternative to closer-in Denver suburbs, attracting both residents and developers as housing costs in core metro areas remain elevated. Details on unit mix and pricing were not immediately available.

The groundbreaking comes amid broader activity in the Denver metro apartment market, where Cherry Creek's 16-block district is reportedly pre-leasing office space at rates 75 percent higher than five years ago, reflecting continued demand for commercial and residential space in the region.

stories.headline: Film on the Rocks announces 2026 season at Red Rocks with 'Sinners,' 'The Princess Bride' and 'Jurassic Park'
stories.summary:
Film on the Rocks has announced its 2026 outdoor movie season at Red Rocks Amphitheatre, featuring a lineup that includes the recent release 'Sinners' alongside classics 'The Princess Bride' and 'Jurassic Park.' The annual series draws large crowds to one of Colorado's most iconic venues for evening screenings against the backdrop of the red sandstone formations.

Film on the Rocks has been a summertime staple in the Denver metro for more than two decades, combining the natural setting of Red Rocks with popular film titles. The full schedule and ticketing information were not immediately detailed in the announcement.

The series typically runs through summer months, offering an alternative entertainment option alongside the amphitheater's busy concert calendar.

weather.notable: About 19 degrees above the average high for this time of year!

joke.joke: I don't look older, I just look worse.

topStory.headline: Trump Arrives in Beijing for First U.S. Presidential Visit to China in Nearly a Decade
topStory.summary:
President Donald Trump arrived in Beijing on Wednesday for a two-day summit with Chinese President Xi Jinping, marking the first visit by a sitting U.S. president to China in nearly a decade. Trump brought a delegation of more than a dozen corporate executives, including Apple CEO Tim Cook, Tesla and SpaceX chief Elon Musk, and Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang, who joined the trip during a stopover in Alaska. The visit is expected to center on trade, artificial intelligence, and Taiwan.

Trade and economic stability are at the top of the agenda, with both governments seeking to reinforce a fragile tariff truce reached last autumn that averted a broader trade war. Trump has long pressed China over its trade surplus with the United States, while Beijing has objected to American export controls and sanctions. Analysts said the summit's primary goal is to establish clearer ground rules for the bilateral economic relationship rather than produce sweeping new agreements.

The summit is taking place against the backdrop of a now three-month-old U.S. conflict with Iran, which has complicated the diplomatic atmosphere. Washington has been pressing Beijing — Iran's largest oil customer and one of the few nations with significant leverage in Tehran — to help reopen the Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly one-fifth of the world's oil supply passes. The U.S. recently sanctioned several Chinese firms accused of assisting Iranian oil shipments, a move Beijing condemned as illegal and countered with a rarely invoked blocking statute.

China has publicly called for stability in the region while stopping short of aligning with Washington. Foreign Minister Wang Yi hosted his Iranian counterpart in Beijing last week and defended Iran's right to civilian nuclear energy development. Xi has offered implicit criticism of U.S. conduct in the conflict, stating that international rule of law must not be selectively applied.

Observers say concerns about Taiwan also loom over the talks, with some analysts warning Trump could be tempted to soften U.S. support for the self-governing island in exchange for Chinese cooperation on Iran. Trump dismissed suggestions that Washington needed Beijing's help in the conflict before departing the White House, saying the U.S. would resolve the situation "peacefully or otherwise." The Beijing summit is described as the first of as many as four planned meetings between Trump and Xi over the coming year.
